content.text: determine which tools it may call and under what conditions. Without an explicit permission model, an agent has the same access as the code that runs it: it can read any file, delete any record, or send any email the system can reach. A permission model narrows that access to what each specific agent actually needs.
the agent is allowed to call (allowlist)
— unconditionally, after confirmation, or only for specific resource IDs

applies: grant each agent only the access it needs to complete its task, revoke access after the task completes, and escalate only when the task genuinely requires it. An agent that can only read files cannot delete them even if a prompt injection attempts to instruct it to do so.
Irreversible data loss or unintended messages

is a callable that accepts a tool name and returns whether the current agent is allowed to call it. The policy is checked at the tool dispatch layer — before the tool function is executed. The agent itself never sees the check; it just receives a
The policy object is kept entirely in the dispatcher, not in the LLM's context. The agent sees tool descriptions but cannot see or modify the policy. When a tool call arrives,
is called first. If it raises, the tool is never invoked and the error is returned as the tool result so the agent knows the call was blocked.
Enforcing Permissions in the Tool Dispatcher

before executing, even if the agent is technically allowed to call them. Send-email, delete-record, and deploy are examples: they are hard to reverse, and a prompt injection attack that tricks the agent into calling them could cause real harm before a human notices.

A flat allowlist does not capture

. An agent might be allowed to read files, but only files in a specific directory. An agent might be allowed to query a database, but only specific tables. Dynamic scoping adds resource constraints to the permission check.

Resource IDs can be file paths, database table names, API endpoint patterns, or any string that identifies the data being accessed. The dispatcher extracts the resource ID from the tool input (e.g., the
: the same tool, called with different inputs, may be allowed or blocked depending on the resource.
Permissions belong in the dispatcher, not the prompt.

Define a permission policy for each agent — which tools it may call, at what level, and whether confirmation is required. Enforce the policy at the tool dispatch layer. Apply least privilege: grant only what the agent needs for the current task. For destructive tools, add a confirmation gate. For fine-grained access, add resource-level scoping. None of this should be visible to the agent — it simply cannot call what it is not permitted to call.
